title: French macarons
subtitle: Almond meringue shells with a ganache or buttercream filling
excerpt: French macarons are small, round almond meringue biscuits sandwiched with ganache or buttercream. The shells should have a smooth, domed top, a ruffled 'foot' at the base, and a chewy centre that yields without crumbling. The technique requires aged egg whites, fine almond flour, and precise folding to the right consistency.
sourceNotes: French macarons in their modern filled form are a twentieth-century Parisian patisserie product, developed at Ladurée and others. The almond-meringue shell itself draws on much older almond biscuit traditions documented from the sixteenth century. The general technique and formula are widely documented in French patisserie texts and are in the public domain.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the assembled macarons in a single layer, then stack with paper between them. Defrost in the fridge overnight.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assembled macarons improve after 24 hours in the fridge; the filling softens the shell to the correct chewy texture.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; the low temperature dries the shells without browning them

content.text: French macarons are made by folding a meringue of aged egg whites and caster sugar into sifted almond flour and icing sugar until the batter falls from the spatula in a slow, thick ribbon. This folding stage (macaronage) is where the outcome is decided: over-fold and the shells spread flat; under-fold and they crack in the oven. A surface skin formed before baking prevents cracking; a 60-minute rest at room temperature does this.
Age the egg whites by separating them 24 to 48 hours ahead and leaving them uncovered in the fridge. Aged whites whip better and produce a more stable meringue.

items.prepNote: finely ground; sift and discard any coarse pieces
aged 24 to 48 hours in the fridge

content.text: Whisk the aged egg whites to soft peaks. Add the caster sugar a tablespoon at a time, whisking to stiff, glossy peaks after each addition. The finished meringue should be thick, white, and hold a firm peak.

Sift the ground almonds and icing sugar together into the meringue. Fold with a rubber spatula using a pressing, smearing motion; not a lifting fold. This intentionally deflates the meringue to the correct consistency. Stop when the batter falls from the spatula in a slow, thick ribbon and the surface evens out in about 10 seconds. At that point it is ready to pipe.

Pipe 4 cm rounds onto lined baking trays. Tap the tray on the work surface several times to flatten peaks and release air bubbles. Leave uncovered at room temperature for 1 hour. The surface should feel dry to the touch before baking; a light skin prevents cracking.

Heat the oven to 150°C fan. Bake for 14 to 16 minutes. The shells should have a smooth dome with a ruffled foot at the base and lift cleanly from the paper when cool. If they stick, they need another 2 minutes. Cool completely before filling.

Heat the cream until steaming. Pour over the chopped chocolate, leave for 1 minute, then stir from the centre until smooth. Cool until thick enough to pipe. Pipe a small amount onto one shell and sandwich with a matching shell. Refrigerate for 24 hours before serving; the rest period is not optional.
